intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking need reassurance and encouragement, not judgment. A warm, cheerleader-like tone reduces friction and shame around kitchen avoidance.
facade.label: Motivational coach vibes
facade.acceptImplies: Voice should be encouraging, use 'you've got this' language, celebrate small wins, normalize shortcuts and cheats
facade.rejectImplies: Voice should stay neutral/instructional, avoid motivational framing, let recipes speak for themselves without emotional scaffolding

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer visual ingredient scanning (photos) over reading text lists, because it feels less like 'reading a recipe' and more like 'grabbing things'
facade.label: Ingredient photos vs ingredient list
facade.acceptImplies: Prioritize camera/photo-based ingredient input and visual ingredient cards over dense ingredient text lists
facade.rejectImplies: Users will engage with structured text lists; design scannable ingredient tables or checkbox formats instead of photo-heavy interfaces

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking need reassurance and encouragement, not judgment. A warm, cheerleader-like tone reduces friction and shame around kitchen avoidance.
evidence.implication: Voice should be encouraging, use 'you've got this' language, celebrate small wins, normalize shortcuts and cheats

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking may prefer designs that feel approachable and non-clinical—soft browns, creams, and warm neutrals rather than stark whites or bright primary colors—to make the app feel less intimidating and more like a trusted friend guiding them through meals.
facade.label: Muted warm tones
facade.acceptImplies: The design should lean into earthy, calming palettes that reduce anxiety around cooking; users respond better to warm, lived-in aesthetics than sterile or overly modern looks.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer either cooler, more clinical color schemes (suggesting efficiency over comfort) or brighter, more playful palettes (suggesting fun over safety), suggesting the app's tone should skew different than 'cozy guide'.

facade.hypothesis: Users feel less intimidated by cooking when the app removes decision-making friction—presenting pre-planned, ingredient-checked meals they can execute in under 15 minutes with minimal steps.
facade.axisTargeted: Calm vs Energetic (reducing cognitive load) + Organic vs Digital (algorithmic curation vs human-curated simplicity)
facade.label: One-tap meals
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into automation, pre-selection, and constraint-based design. Users want the app to think FOR them, not just organize options.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want agency and exploration despite hating cooking. Avoid over-automation; they may prefer discovery, variety, or flexibility over prescriptive meal plans.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer recipes revealed incrementally (one step at a time, full-screen) over seeing the entire recipe upfront
facade.axisTargeted: Information density and pacing—whether to show everything at once (dense, scrollable list) or gate it (sparse, sequential reveal)
facade.label: One-tap meals
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into step-by-step UI with minimal cognitive load per screen. Could support timer integration, ingredient checkoff flow, hands-free voice navigation.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want recipe overview before committing. Shift toward card-based recipe browser, ingredient preview, time/difficulty badges visible upfront. Full recipe visible on one scrollable page.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer fast, snappy interactions (instant taps, quick results) over animated transitions that feel slow or precious
facade.label: Instant vs smooth
facade.acceptImplies: Build with crisp, zero-delay feedback. Taps land immediately. No decorative animations. Speed signals 'this respects your time.'
facade.rejectImplies: Smooth transitions and fluid motion matter. Even a recipe app for busy people benefits from breathing room and visual continuity between states.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to irreverent, witty banter that validates their frustration rather than pretending cooking is fun. A sarcastic tone creates complicity—'I get it, this sucks'—which builds trust faster than cheerleading.
facade.label: Sarcastic sidekick
facade.acceptImplies: The app should lean into dark humor, self-deprecating jokes, and knowing asides. Examples: 'This recipe has 47 steps. Just kidding, it's 3. We lied.' or 'Cooking is terrible. Let's do it anyway.'
facade.rejectImplies: Users find sarcasm off-brand or alienating. They prefer straightforward, warm, or neutral tone over cynicism. The app should avoid being the 'cool kid' and focus on being genuinely helpful instead.

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so simple, you'll actually make them

Filter by mood
Today's picks
Garlic butter pasta
8 min 4 ingredients
Avocado toast
3 min 3 ingredients
Tuna salad wrap
5 min 5 ingredients
